title: Guard against empty collections
content: Functions that index into a sequence, divide by its length, or call max()/min()/statistics on it must handle the empty case explicitly. Decide the contract for empty input (return a neutral value such as 0 or None, or raise a clear error) instead of letting ZeroDivisionError or ValueError surface.

title: Avoid mutable default arguments
content: Default argument values are evaluated once when the function is defined, so a default list or dict is shared across all calls and accumulates state. Use None as the default and create a fresh object inside the function body: def f(x, items=None): if items is None: items = [].

title: Validate inputs and handle None / missing keys
content: Do not assume inputs are present and well-formed. Check for None before calling methods or operators on a value, and use dict.get with a default rather than direct key access when a key may be absent. Fail with a clear message or a safe default instead of an AttributeError or KeyError.

title: Handle out-of-range and boundary inputs
content: Indexing and slicing assume elements exist. Before accessing items[i], confirm the index is in range, or provide a fallback. Pay attention to off-by-one boundaries and the smallest/largest valid inputs, which are the most common source of defects.

title: Validate state-changing operations before applying them
content: Operations that change critical state — payments, balance changes, deletions, transfers — must validate their preconditions before mutating anything and fail safely when a precondition is not met. For example, a withdrawal must reject an amount greater than the available balance rather than producing a negative balance.

title: Never build queries or commands by string concatenation
content: Concatenating untrusted input directly into SQL, shell, or other command strings enables injection attacks. Use parameterized queries or a safe builder/escaping API so that input is always treated as data, never as executable syntax.

title: Prefer efficient lookups and avoid needless nested loops
content: Membership tests against a list are O(n); converting to a set or dict makes them O(1). Nested loops over the same collection are O(n^2) and often replaceable with a single pass or a set-based check. Optimize only while preserving behavior, and confirm the result is unchanged.
